comments.title: Working feedback?
comments.content:
I need to increase awareness of my company's brand, and at the moment, we're utilizing social media and reaching out to media outlets to get coverage. I created the below working to enhance these strategies- could you let me know how you think it could be improved? My inspiration was your earlier suggestion of using correspondences between the planets and the Gates of the SOP.

Open Grove (DMH). Place open jar of cool, purified water on altar.

"By the powers of the Second Gate, the planet Mercury, and in the great name MANNANAN, may this water enhance and lubricate communication, bringing knowledge of [Brand Name] to receptive minds far and wide."

Trace the invoking symbol of the Second Gate over the water, then visualize intensely the blue light of the second gate flowing from the gate, through the smaller triangle, and filling the jar more and more brightly with blue light. When finished, draw the awen symbol over the jar, and chant AWEN three times.

Close Grove, and carefully wrap the jar of water and store away.

Then throughout the following week, when I make a social media post, or email a media outlet or blogger, during the planetary hour of Mercury, I take a sip of the water, trace the invoking symbol of the second gate over the computer screen or maybe the mouse, and imagine the blue light flow through my hand and into the computer.

What do you think? I could possibly upgrade this by using an herbal potion instead of just water. If used regularly, could this cause an elemental imbalance within me?

replies.title: Re: Working feedback?
replies.content:
That's quite functional. You've got a single purpose, a coherent symbolism, and a way to ground out the energy in action in the material plane. Yes, you could unbalance yourself if you do that working and only that working for a long time, but you can prevent that easily enough by (a) doing the full Sphere of Protection working daily and (b) doing other practical workings from time to time.

I'd stick with pure water rather than an herbal potion, since the symbolism of the working focuses on water. Give it a try and see how your results work out!

replies.title: Re: Working feedback?
replies.content:
Just a thought.

You may want to go take a walk or put your conscious focus else where after you drink the water and hold off posting for a few minute. Mercury may just inspire better or more effective copy or content ideas via your subconscious.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello and a happy Magic Monday to you once again Mr. Greer!

I was reading through some of the discussion from last week and came across your comment that messing with the spirits of the dead is liable to turn out disastrously. This called back to my mind something I'd forgotten. Last year, on the Sunday before Halloween, I went to what is called an "ecstatic dance" class, which is essentially a space where for a couple of hours a bunch of New Agey types gather to shake their bodies about in an exploratory manner which can only be called dancing in the loosest sense of the word, accompanied by a mix of techno and World music, in typical New Age grab-bag fashion. I used to be a regular at these things, being something of a recovering New Ager myself; by this time I was pretty thoroughly disillusioned with much of New Age philosophy, but still found enough enjoyment and value in these classes to attend every now and again.

Anyway, the way this particular class went, the music and dancing started off gently and progressively picked up in tempo and intensity until it reached an ecstatic peak. At this point, with the energy in the room at its height and, crucially, I believe, for what followed, everyone at their most suggestible, the facilitator of the class made the suggestion that, since it was Halloween season, if we so desired we might at this point call upon a deceased relative to "dance with" us. I'd like to say I thought better, but I was so swept away by the intensity of the moment that I decided to go along.

At first I called upon the spirit of my Grandmother, but after not really feeling anything from this my mind latched onto the idea of calling upon my Grandfather, which made me very excited and so, raising my arms above my head and drawing them down over my body in a spontaneous ritual act, as if summoning him in, I strongly willed him to come and dance with me.

I should say that my Grandfather committed suicide when my mother was a young teenager and I never met him. However, for much of my life I've felt a curious kinship to him, partly I think because of my own past struggles with depression, but also partly because of my troubled relationship with my mother growing up where, having reflected on it a great deal, I believe she projected a lot of her feelings about him onto me, causing me to unconsciously identify with him. I believe I've now processed a lot of the issues surrounding this, but at the time of this dance class I was only just beginning to do so and so my Grandfather still had a strong grip on my imagination.

I can't remember very clearly what happened next, but I recall undergoing a definite shift, where I suddenly welled up with an unexpected intensity of grief and pain, expressing itself through my dancing which became really chaotic and aggressive. Though this all faded somewhat by the end of the class, I was left feeling more than a bit uneasy, perhaps because it really felt that I'd made some kind of contact and with a very troubled spirit at that. Reflecting more on it over the next few days, I came to think it had been an extremely stupid thing to do and I became worried that I might have disturbed my Grandfather's spirit and I was in for serious trouble.

However, there was no noticeable trouble after that initial incident, hence I suppose how I was able to forget about the whole thing until reading your comment last week. When I read that comment I took it as a dire judgement; it came back to me what I'd done and I felt very angry with myself, not just for the danger I'd risked incurring on myself, but also for how narcissistic it now struck me it was to attempt to invoke my Grandfather (and Grandmother) in such circumstances. I guess I had in mind that it would be some kind of catharsis, so it wasn't quite so frivolous as the idea of calling upon a dead spirit to dance with you might appear on the surface, but it was still extremely self-centred, with little to no consideration of what my relatives' spirits, as conscious entities unto themselves, might want or need for themselves. (It's a very common mentality in the New Age and one which I now find fairly distasteful, even offensive, to believe, like a child, that everything and everybody in the world exists only for your personal amusement; I believe my actions were, at least in part, a reflection of this.)

I felt rather anxious about this over the next few days and felt that I needed to make it right somehow, yet was wary of further meddling. However, it got to a point where I couldn't control myself and I addressed them both, in the form of internal prayers, saying I was sorry for being so selfish as to call on them like I did and I wanted them to know I had no desire to continue to disturb their business in whatever realms their souls inhabited. I don't know if this was particularly advisable but as I said I couldn't control myself, overcome by anxiety and regret. I didn't engage in any kind of formal ceremony, these were just prayers uttered in my head, so I don't know if they would have even been received, although I realised afterwards it was on Halloween night, which I suppose might have made it more likely?

In any case, though I have still not experienced any indications of trouble from either the initial incident nor this latest, panic-driven attempted contact, I feel I should consult somebody who knows something about these matters, in case I've done something more horribly wrong than it appears and any kind of action is required. So if you or any of your readers can offer a response, that would be very welcome. I believe I may have made contact with at least one and possibly both of the relatives concerned and I can't imagine they'd be too pleased about, but I hope the apparent lack of reaction from them shows they love me enough to forgive my idiocy on this occasion. It does slightly spook me that all this stuff should have reemerged on Halloween, having first occurred on the previous Halloween and been absent from my thoughts all the time in between; if anybody can offer any insights into this, they would also be welcome.

Best wishes, Albert

Dear JMG

replies.title: Re: Night Terrors
replies.content:
A basic protective ritual such as the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram or the Sphere of Protection, done in your son's bedroom, will likely help; you can also make a potpourri of protective herbs (St. John's wort is particularly useful for this sort of thing) and leave it in a bowl in the bedroom.

replies.title: Re: Night Terrors
replies.content:
That was me, Morven, aged 7-8 or thereabouts, seventy-odd years ago. I couldn't put my dreams into words, either, though I knew very well what had happened in them. It was simply that putting the dream into words would make it too vivid for me to bear, and would have intensified my terror to the point where I could not speak or hear words at all. The terrible memory of those dreams tied my tongue, and nearly stopped up my ears.

My mother was finally fed up with waking up every night to comfort me. So she taught me a method to deal with them. She said, roughly, that I should pay attention to the process by which I fell asleep, and look for that brief moment when I was almost asleep, but still dimly aware that I was lying in my bed. In that moment look around inside your mind, she said, and you will see the start of your first dream like a picture that has not yet begun to change and move to make a story. Decide whether it looks like the beginning of a scary dream or a fun one.

If the picture looks like the start of a scary dream, pull back just a little so you can see how that picture has been drawn on a chalkboard inside your mind. Now every chalkboard comes with an eraser, so all you have to do is to pick up that eraser and erase the picture from the chalkboard. Then that dream is gone, and it won't be your first dream--maybe it won't be any dream--that night.

Do this every night, every time you're awake and starting to fall asleep. It will take a while to get really good at it.

Once you can do that easily, the next step it to take up the colored chalks that come with the chalkboard, and draw the first picture of the dream you want to have on the chalkboard, where it can replace the scary dream you were going to have if you hadn't erased its first picture.

It took me about six months, as I remember, to get good at the first step; the second step came somewhat more easily after that.

This was my first--in someways, my most important--lesson in magic. It was my mother's family that had esoteric interests, and with one shining exception her male ancestors (ever since her great-grandmother's day) treated their womenfolk horridly. So the attitude among the women was that magic was for women only. Why give the men another weapon? They would only use it against their women ...

If it had not been for those dreams, terrifying beyond any ability (then) to put into words, I might never have learned any of my mother's family's practices. But, as I said, she was desperate for uninterrupted sleep, and that broke the dam somewhat. (Even so, I do not think she taught me most of what she knew.)

replies.title: Re: Night Terrors
replies.content: Thank you, Robert. That's a very good tip that I will certainly try. Your mother sounds like quite a remarkable woman.

replies.title: Re: Night Terrors
replies.content:
You are welcome! The women in that line for four generations before me were certainly remarkable, each in her own way; but they were all very, very difficult people, too. They all "lay athwart the currents of their times."

I think one generally learns more from difficult, damaged, odd-ball ancestors than one ever can learn from sweet, loving, benevolent, untraumatized ones. Now that I am elderly and can better understand the family history, I would not trade off that set of ancestors for any other.

comments.title: Bottle Spells
comments.content:
Good morning John Michael,
I have a couple of questions on Hoodoo Bottle Spells.

It is said that once the jar is closed we cannot open it lest we undo the spell.
And it must be disposed of as it is.

Now, Hoodoo Ammonia Bottle Spells uses undiluted ammonia which is very toxic. Lucky Mojo says: “...Be careful, when disposing of Ammonia, to thoroughly dilute it with water...”
How do we dispose of it?

One fellow’s site says you can open the jar throw the ammonia out and burn the paper once it is dry. Can we do so?
Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Bottle Spells
replies.content: Okay, this is a useful teaching moment. You will find contradictory advice on every possible question about magic, and if you take it all seriously you'll end up in a fine state of paralysis. If you're following a specific set of instructions, follow those instructions and don't worry if "it is said" to do something that conflicts with them. The ammonia bottle spell explains that you leave the bottle alone until the spell has done its work; once it's done its work, in turn, yes, you can dilute the ammonia (pouring it in a thin stream down your sink while the water's running will do this), dry the paper, and dispose of the remnants. I'd bury them in the back yard rather than burning the paper, as this helps lock the spell's effects into place.

comments.title: Native American Spiritual Protection

Hello JMG

Why were the Native Americans not better protected, against the colonists, by their spirituality? Their spirituality seems pretty powerful to me, and I'm surprised it didn't help them more.

SMJ

replies.title: Re: Native American Spiritual Protection
replies.content:
Spirituality isn't a solution for every problem. No matter how good you are at magic, you will still have disappointments and bad days, and eventually you and the people you care about will die. The same is true on a larger scale: you can spend your life working magic for a cause you deeply believe in, and the cause can still fail.

Magic is no more omnipotent than any other human capacity. It works often enough to be worth doing, but there are things it can't do.

(BTW: the fact that *many* Amerindians were each others' enemies and eager to ally with Europeans "didn't hurt".)

- S. T. Silva.

comments.title: Re: Working with the fae
comments.content:
There is some degree of overlap between the fae, the dead, and elementals. On their own, each of these categories have its own risks.

This is an area where you do not want to be a guinea pig. To not leave you in the dark, here are some books on work with the fairies. Note that I am not endorsing these books; I think work with fairies is a bad idea.

Nature Spirits and Elemental Beings, by Marko Pogačnik
The Tree of Enchantment, by Orion Foxwood.
Fifty Years in the Feri Tradition, by Cora Anderson.
The R. J. [Robert John] Stewart books on the subject.

You should also read about them in folklore books. Incidentally, this would also be a bad idea. They would notice your attention and starting to pay visits. You start like John Keel, writing Jadoo and then you end neck deep in the Mothman affair and running from the Men in Black.

comments.title: Seasons
comments.content:
I have two questions:

1. You once mentioned that there were seasons that were good for certain life changes, can you remind me of the seasons, or point me in the correct direction for study?

2. On that same subject, I seem to remember fall is a good time for change. I went to England in mid-September (a place that has always helped me re-charge and change course), and returned to CA the day after the autumn equinox. That week I was contacted to interview for a new job at the Uni where I work. I'm sure you don't remember, but I am currently on a team of 4 and being bullied relentlessly by my manager and co-worker, and I truly believe my co-worker (who claims to be an Aztec priestess) has been using some sort of magic against me (either on purpose or subconsciously, is that possible?). Well, I got the job in a different department and along with it a big promotion and raise. I'm still in shock and can hardly believe it after enduring a year of manipulation and bullying that left me physically and mentally ill. My question: In this situation are there any rituals, readings, or anything else you recommend to prepare for my new job? I am really worried that I will take some of my fear, anxiety or dark feelings and emotions to this new job, and I'd really like to start off positive and healthy.

Thank you,
Tude

replies.title: Re: Seasons
replies.content:
1) Spring is the tide of beginnings and growth; summer, the tide of completion and fruitfulness; autumn, the tide of preservation and taking stock; and winter, the tide of destruction and purification.

2) Take a hoodoo bath for protection and purification -- if you don't recall the formula, I'll post it below. Once you've finished the bath and poured some of the water onto the roots of a strong tree, light a white candle and sit before it for a time. Then say seven times: "I welcome my new job and the new hopes and possibilities it brings, and I release all the fear, anxiety, and troubled emotions that my old job brought with it."

Do this whole process -- the bath followed by the candle and the affirmation -- seven times before you start the new job, and you'll be fine.

Yes, people can do nasty magic sub- or semi-consciously. That's one of the ugly realities that lies behind the old legends of witchcraft.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What was different about medieval and renaissance times that made it ethical for diviners to tell people about their deaths then, but not now?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A radically different attitude toward death. People in the Middle Ages and Renaissance were comfortable with the fact that they were going to die; there were books about ars moriendi, the art of dying, which taught the literate how to get ready for death and how to go through the process without freaking out or causing anybody unnecessary suffering; those who couldn't read got the same advice from folklore and from their religious professionals. Thus finding out when you were going to die wasn't traumatic at all; you had an appointment with the Grim Reaper, just as you might have an appointment with anyone else.

Now? People in the industrial world go into hysterics at the thought of getting noticeably old. In our present culture, knowing the date of their own death would as likely as not cause people to plunge into severe depression or florid psychosis. Thus it's not ethical to tell them, just as you don't go around needlessly triggering any other mentally fragile person.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This would seem to imply that if someone is comfortable with death, and asks, you can tell them. Is that correct?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Only if you have very good reason to know that the person actually is comfortable with death. There's a lot of pretense around such things these days.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I didn't use any tools beyond writing and drawing on paper. I never got the fabled 'perfect site integration' where it's just like being at the target. When I did get the best connections with targets I felt more like I'd brought the target to me than gone there. Occasionally I'd get visual glimpses, but it was more like knowing it was there than seeing it.

The most distinctive sensory connection I ever got was viewing an Arienne 5 launch when I got the same rumbling felling in the belly I'd got at an airshow when a Vulcan bomber took off. Another time I perfectly described a fountain sculpture in Zipaquira Salt Cathedral. The most intense emotion I ever got was when I asked "what is the purpose of this target?" and before I could even finish the question I got "WINNING". It was the aftermath of the bombing of Goole in the Blitz.

Sometimes it was funny stuff like getting a target that was "loud and obnoxious but also pink and girly". I was almost certain it was a car (or possibly powerboat) getting down to it having a V12 engine. Turned out to be a pink Ferrari (with a V12 engine). The strangest though was a building I described as 'verbose' and got 'Ovid' and 'elegiac'. It was a building with a Latin inscription over the door, but it was Horace and hexameter. I also now know the 19th Century Kendal Gaslight Festival wasn't just a great party, but to those there it was miraculous, it was a new world.

But most of the time it was descriptions and images that I had no idea what I was describing (which is how it's supposed to work so you're imagination doesn't run away with you). 'Raised lines receding into the distance' turns out to be a railway station. One time I got 'Piper Alpha' but it was a giant float at the Rio carnival in the shape of an oil rig. I also discovered it's possible to get three targets in a row where there's someone in a suit of armour, even if you can't believe it at the time. :)

comments.title: Deriving meaning from a vision
comments.content:
The talk of spirits within the land and Vine Deloria Jr's God Is Red has made me think a lot about a brief vision, or contact with the spirit realm I had about a decade ago. I was sitting along a creek, enjoying the sun and peaceful outdoors, trying to meditate. Suddenly a woman whom I can only describe as being Native American in appearance came floating over the creek towards me. She wore a headdress of a frog. As she neared me, the word Nahautl was felt to emanate out of her. I snapped to attention and she disappeared. The experience was very powerful. I've never known what to do with it. I feel there is some meaning which I should derive from it. How does one go about finding the meaning from such experiences? Any direction would be most grateful!

Thanks,
Prizm

replies.title: Re: Deriving meaning from a vision
replies.content:
If you'll pardon me taking your question in a slightly different direction? Because that experience is fascinating.

Nahautl is a word I recognized immediately from my past readings on the Aztecs, but I remembered it as referring to something other than a deity. Checking Wiki indicates I was correct - it's the name of the Aztec language. Some obvious possibilities given that are that Someone is either now answering to that name or always answered to it and the knowledge was lost with the Spanish, or that she was checking to see if you spoke the language.

I use the capitalization for a reason; that very much sounds like a visitation from a deity, and given the use of "Nahautl" almost certainly a Mesoamerican one. Which brings me to the other reason this story caught my attention: given that frog headdress, I rather suspect this goddess would answer to either Kek or Kauket, even if it's not her usual name...

Roughly where was this experience, if you don't mind me asking?

replies.title: Re: Deriving meaning from a vision
replies.content:
I actually did research it right away, and finding the word belonged to the language of the peoples in Mesoamerica even before the Aztecs I started learning a bit about the mythology and stories of the region. It never progressed from there as I let other things in life get in the way.

This experience happened in Northeastern Minnesota not terribly far from the Boundary Waters. If it helps, I was born and raised in Texas.

replies.title: Re: Deriving meaning from a vision
replies.content:
I'd be very surprised if Mesoamerica extends that far north, so it's unlikely to be just a contact with a local deity. I suppose it's possible that a Mesoamerican goddess could take the role of tamanous, but the first possibility that comes to my mind is that you were being called home.

Born Texan could definitely be important, but it's less informative than I would like; I was just talking on the other blog about how I think there's at least one boundary running through the state. Which part of Texas? (Hill Country, northeastern Piney Woods, southeast Texas, Rio Grande Valley, West Texas, Great Plains, North Central Plain?)

replies.title: Re: Deriving meaning from a vision
replies.content: According to their own traditions, the Aztecs came to Mexico from Aztlan, a land of seven caves, very far to the north of their historic homeland.  People who speculate on where Aztlan might have been usually don't look as far north and east as Minnesota, but I suppose one can't entirely rule out the possibility.  Nahuatl belongs to the Uto-Aztecan family of Native American languages, and those relatives of Nahuatl are attested even today as far north as Idaho and Wyoming; in the distant past, their reach may have been wider.

replies.title: Re: Deriving meaning from a vision
replies.content:
There's been quite a few signs calling me home. It wouldn't surprise me if this were the case.

As far as Texas, I was born in the Rio Grande Valley, spent most of my formative years in Southeast Texas ( Conroe, Montgomery, Beaumont) and spent the last five years in the Permian Basin. It's always been hard to call a specific place home. :D

replies.title: Re: Deriving meaning from a vision
replies.content: I would be shocked if the Rio Grande Valley isn't fundamentally in Mesoamerica's ambit, and the Beaumont area probably is as well, so that makes sense.  I'd guess there's fluidity in the borders, but this isn't likely to be a case where it applies.

replies.title: Re: Deriving meaning from a vision
replies.content:
My first thought was the same as Username's -- the word "Nahuatl" is the name of the most widely spoken language in native Mexico. I'd encourage you to research Mesoamerican mythology and religion, and see if you can find a goddess or spirit who corresponds with your vision. Once you have that knowledge you can proceed from there.

Where did this happen, by the way?

replies.title: Re: Deriving meaning from a vision
replies.content:

At the time I was trying to research things I stumbled upon a place, Guanajuato, which roughly translated as "place of the frogs". I don't believe that meaning was in the Nahuatl language though and life threw me some curveballs taking me elsewhere for some years. Recently I've had the word thrown around at me again in ways which seem like a gentle reminder.

The place this experience happened was in Northeastern Minnesota not too far from the Boundary Waters, and if it helps any, I was born and raised in Texas. I get the impression that the gods have connections and have ways to accomplish what they want.

These days, most everyone dealing with Fortean lore, folklore, ghost stories, high strangeness, haunted history, the paranormal, and cryptids are running podcasts. UFOs may be out of fashion, but it seems that people are still having strange experiences and sharing them.

I started poking around that scene for story ideas over the last few months (and also because I have a desk job, so, hey, why not listen to some stories?), and found myself stumbling into what seems to be the beginning of a possible resurgence. I think with all the tensions, violence, anger, fear, etc, there's also the uptick in weird stuff happening. I also believe that people here just aren't sharing their stories too openly; they're afraid they'll be attacked/disbelieved, so you won't hear them talking outside the podcasts and websites, or face-to-face over a drink as the fire's dying down. That most people don't have the contexts or spiritual/emotional/cognative tools to reflect on, process, and deal with their own experiences seems to be a common theme woven throughout. It's a sad lack in most of modern American culture.

For monsters in modern times specifically, there's a podcast that's collecting listener stories "The Monsters Among Us". For theory/discussion type stuff, "Where Did The Road Go" might suit your purposes?

The rest of the podcast may or may not be of interest to you, but Astonishing Legends did a multi-part deep dive on the Black Eyed Kids that turned out to be fascinating.

Jim Harold's Campfire is a madly popular podcast that's been collecting listener stories for years (he also runs The Paranormal Podcast) but it skews hard toward ghost stories, as that's those seem to be the experiences people have most often and/or are most willing to call in and share.

First, get an enormous green candle, a big square quart sized one, since this working could take years and years, and a lottery ticket for a Wednesday a month from now (lottery tickets can usually be purchased a month in advance).

Then, Friday before sunrise, tear a square of brown paper from a bag and write your name seven times,

then turn the paper a quarter turn and write over your name seven times,
100 good jobs plus one
100 good jobs plus one
100 good jobs pus one
100 good jobs plus one
100 good jobs plus one
100 good jobs plus one
100 good jobs plus one
fold the paper in half, folding it towards you, then turn it a quarter turn and fold it towards you again, so it's folded in quarters.

Then, at dawn exactly, put it and the lottery ticket under the green candle, light the candle, and say "I am willing to do whatever I have to do, without harm to myself or anyone else, to create a hundred good jobs plus one for myself." Let the candle burn for seven minutes, and then snuff it out --don't blow it out. Burn it again each Friday morning.

And if you're part of the other fifth (there's overlap in the first two, the complement is pretty large), and you have to learn by doing, then perhaps you could prepare a Shark Tank presentation for a marketing class.
The second half is to act- to volunteer somewhere where you can learn some skill necessary in business, for example, help out with Bingo Night at the local night, or help with charity vending at a local college or high school basketball game.

Each month, you purchase another ticket in advance and use any small winnings towards this project.

Good Luck!

comments.title: Pet protection
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer:
I have a new cat, over the last year we "lost" 3 cats, the last one was our dearest "Mother cat", she was 5 years old, so she wasn't old enough and the others were kittens. Looking for her we learned that there is someone in the neighborhood poisoning cats (all of ours are sterilized so they are not aggressive not sexually noisy), so this neighbor thinks this is the reason of our losses.
The thing is that we love our cats a lot and we would like to give them some protection spell or amulet...
Is there anything we could do?

Thanks a lot!

replies.title: Re: Pet protection
replies.content:
Under the circumstances you describe, the most effective protection would be to keep your cats inside. This will work if your will is stronger than the cats'.

I think it is rather sad to confine cats indoors all the time; they have natural behaviors that they can't enjoy indoors and they get a certain amount of nature deficit disorder, just like human beings. If you have a backyard or a deck, there is a compromise. You can buy or construct what's being called a "catio", a screened porch that keeps the cat from roaming but allows her to watch birds, smell the fresh air, sleep in a patch of sunlight, etc.

In addition, were I in this situation, I would also be doing a spell to bring the cat poisoner to the attention of the animal protection authorities and the police (once I could get my rage under control). Apart from his cruelty to animals and their guardians, people who abuse or murder animals for pleasure sometimes move on to abusing and murdering human beings.

Or if I couldn't manage a spell, I would pray to a deity who is a protector of cats (Bast) or animals in general (Artemis), ask for protection and welfare of my cats, and also mention that I have heard that someone nearby is poisoning cats. I'd leave it up to the deity what to do about that. In the meantime, I would keep my cats under physical guard.

I might also attempt to do protective magic for the other pets in the neighborhood. But this kind of magic is not one hundred percent reliable, and the perpetrator might move on to killing wild animals or animals outside the neighborhood, or homeless people . . .

I don't do binding spells. You might get advice on binding spells from someone else.

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content:
Good! As the old adage has it, "the wise govern their stars, the foolish are governed by them." One of the main points of knowing your horoscope is that you can take the hard aspects in your chart -- squares, oppositions, etc. -- and work with them constructively, instead of being controlled by them.

As for not getting stuck with the purely psychological end of astrology, that's not hard at all; find at least one book on reading a natal chart from before 1950 or so, and work through your natal chart with that as well. The older books include predictions about how your life will work out, etc.

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content: That sounds really useful. Why were those dropped in more recent work?

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content: Because when astrology became fashionable among middle-class Americans in the 1960s and 1970s, they didn't want to be told that they couldn't have everything they wanted -- and astrology will pretty reliably tell you that. The astrology they were willing to pay for was the kind that would tell them all about themselves (cue opera singer belting out "me, me, me, mememememeME!"), and so that's what got published in books and taught by schools that wanted their graduates to be able to support themselves. Astrology is no more immune from the laws of supply and demand than anything else.

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content:
Yes, in fact im using Alan Leo books on the matter.

But what i would like to know is if learning to rule your stars mitigate some of the destiny effects Leo anounces in his books.It seems to me that there are parts that can be integrated, and still others that must be accepted, and it is one that should plan his life in order not to trigger that weakness.

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content: Yes, that's a pretty good summary of it. Exactly what you can mitigate is a complicated question. You always have the choice of accepting the limitation -- for example, if your seventh house has a malefic in it in bad aspect to the ruler of the seventh, who's not rescued by favorable aspects or placements, you can simply decide not to get married and save yourself some serious grief. Alternatively, you can decide to work on balancing the energies of a difficult placement or aspect -- for example, if you have Mars square the ruler of your ascendant, you can work on controlling your temper; you'll still get the sudden bursts of anger but you can learn to harness them instead of just letting them mess you over.

comments.title: Bread diety
comments.content: I am a bread baker, sometimes by profession and other times for enjoyment and providing good food for my family. If I would like to thank and ask for blessing on my baking from any diety related to bread, how would you recommend doing that? From my research, it seems like Demeter is the main bread goddess that I can find, but I am very unfamiliar with Greek dieties and what is the traditional way to approach them. I am also practicing druidry; which of the druid dieties could I thank/invoke for bread baking?

replies.title: Re: Bread deity
replies.content: Any earth goddess is appropriate to invoke for blessings on the products of the earth. In Druidry, that would be Ced the Earth Mother if you follow the Revival traditions, or Danu if you're into the Irish lore.

replies.title: Re: Bread deity
replies.content:
In the myths associated with the Eleusinian Mysteries, I believe Triptolemos brought cereal agriculture (or maybe the plow) to the people. I haven't read up much on him but he seems to fall in the category of divinized heroes, demigods and legendary ancestors.

You can probably find the Homeric Hymn to Demeter online. She is a barley goddess, a goddess of cereal agriculture in general, and a mother goddess of favorable aspect as long as you approach Her respectfully and aren't harming Her daughter Persephone. Beer made from barley would be a good libation to offer Demeter. Greek statues depict Demeter as a dignified, fully dressed woman with a matronly but not corpulent figure.

As the word "cereal" implies, Ceres is the Roman counterpart to Demeter.

If a Victorian cameo has come down in your family, and it depicts the head of a woman with flowing locks, look for sheaves of wheat in her hair. If you see any, you are looking at a cameo of Ceres.

If there are grape leaves, she's a Bacchante. If there are butterfly wings in the hair or on her back, she's Psyche. If she is in Greco-roman dress, has a stern expression and is wearing a tiara, she is Juno. If she has a little crescent in her hair, she is Diana. If she's wearing a helmet, she's Minerva. If there are flowers in her hair, she's either Flora or just a pretty woman. Two beautiful women in profile, if one has downcast eyes and there is an owl near her, you are looking at a personification of Night and Day.
-----------
The Jewish blessing for bread on the Sabbath and other holidays, translates to:

{This opening is formulaic for many blessings}
Blessed are You, O Lord our God, King of the Universe,

{This is the specific part of the blessing}
Who brings forth bread from the earth.

This blessing is said before you eat the bread.

replies.title: Re: Protection and being a public occultist
replies.content:
I haven't had anyone publicly announce that they're going to fling nasty magic my way. That would be entertaining! I do field what looks like hostile magic tolerably often; as a public figure in the occult community who routinely takes controversial stands, that's inevitable.

Regular banishings really do take care of most of it; the Rose Cross ritual or its equivalents are also very good to use; there's a range of hoodoo techniques in which I'm tolerably skilled, and as I mentioned earlier in these comments, if you do enough meditation, you get to the point that you can sense the hostile energy and neutralize it without having to resort to ritual at all. (As far as I know this can't be taught; you learn it by doing enough meditation that it suddenly becomes obvious.) I also have several other protective options that, for obvious reasons, I don't talk about.

I don't recommend the "Dog of Evil" ritual. That's one of Crowley's antics, and yeah, you'll probably end up getting bitten.

replies.title: Re: Branching Out

comments.title: Meditation
comments.content:
A few days ago I was going for a walk in the nearby forest. Following a sudden impulse I decided to meditate while walking. My intention was to just listen to the forest and see if I can get some connection. I started with green color breathing and turned "idle" afterwards, listening. I silently walked on for a while, alert and only very few distractive thoughts. After a while I noticed that my vision changed. Normally, if something moves in the periphery of your vision you don't really see it but immediately move your eyes and possibly turn the head to focus it (at least I do most of the time). But now, there was no need to focus anything since I could consciously observe everything within my full field of vision (a bit like vision at night but in full colours, much sharper and the field of vision even wider. As an example: The rain was pouring and in almost 180° I could see all the leaves moving when a drop hit them at the same time. I stopped and stood still, just looking.
After a while something else happened. It seemed as if I was looking at the scene through a very thin veil of fog. At the same time I felt differently - I don't know how to best describe it. The beginning of a "whiteout" is maybe exaggerated but I believe letting it intensify would have meant a loss of control to a certain degree.
I stopped that and stayed the meditative state I reached before. After another while a thought came to my mind - how old this forest is and how deep its roots, even though all the trees around me were rather young. I thought of all the anger there must be since the forest must have suffered a lot over the last few centuries (150 years ago it was cleared almost entirely). Then it was as if the forest was answering me - no anger, but deep equanimity (I experienced something like this a few years ago. A large beech I liked very much and visited regularly was hewn one day. I was sad and angry but immediately a thought like "don't worry mate, everything is fine" entered my mind).

Long story, some questions:
1) Is it wise to do meditation in the way I did? Is there a possibility that If I run around and just listen, someone may come to talk I'd rather not talk to? What to do in such a case?
2) Any thoughts on the visual experience?
3) What to make of that foggy / whiteout / loss of control experience? Is this something common? Something to avoid? Is there anything I could make out of it if I wanted?
5) In context to 1): In another post you wrote about the possibility of using meditation for defence. Does this mean you can use meditation instead of hoodoo baths and protective rituals? Where and how could I learn about that possibility?

I could go on for some more pages, but will leave it like this for now...

Thank you very much, as always!
Nachtgurke

2) None in particular. It's not something I've had.

3) That's also not something I've experienced. See if it happens again, and ask the nearest tree what to do about it.

replies.title: Re: Meditation
replies.content:
I have some experience with the visual experience you describe from my background in wilderness skills, tracking, and martial arts.

From what I can tell, the mode of visual perception you describe is the natural mode for human beings who have grown up predominantly outdoors in a natural environment. It is what the tracker Tom Brown Jr. sometimes calls “wide-angle vision.” What most modern (i.e. domesticated, indoor-type) humans think of as normal vision is actually an artifact of the unnatural environment in which we are raised.

There are three main regions of visual acuity in humans: foveal, macular, and peripheral. The fovea is the center of the visual range, packed with cones and being the area of our sharpest focus. This is the part of our vision that we naturally use for tasks such as threading a needle or reading.

The macula (which technically includes the fovea at its middle) is the area of the retina that provides the main 30-40 degree cone of fairly good focus at the center of our visual arc. It is a mix of rods and cones, providing a balance of color and shape/edge detection.

Peripheral vision is provided by the area of the retina surrounding the macula. It is much higher in rods and lower in cones, making it good at detecting shape and movement but less able to see color accurately. This is why peripheral vision is better for seeing in low light. Peripheral vision typically extends about 180 degrees left-to-right and somewhat less than 180 degrees up-down (limited by the presence of the brow and cheekbones).

Each of these areas of the retina is wired to a different part of the brain that is responsible for processing and interpreting their sensory inputs. The foveal and macular parts of our vision give us the visual information required to carry out everyday tasks while the peripheral is designed to be sensitive to motion so that it can warn us of danger (such as a predator stalking us in the grass) or alert us to the presence of animals that might represent food.

Thus people growing up in a natural environment (especially those in areas where the local fauna is potentially dangerous) have a strong incentive to fully develop their brain’s ability to process the information being fed in through peripheral vision. Those who seriously practice the martial arts also have good reason to develop this ability since it provides critical information about the environment during battle.

If fact, every indigenous people group that I have worked with over the years has an extremely well developed visual acuity that includes paying careful attention to information coming from all parts of the eye. The description given above is precisely how native peoples seem to experience the world as a normal part of their visual sensory experience.

The San Bushman trackers from Africa are some of the most profound examples of this I have seen. With the substantial number of potentially lethal species in their region and the fact that they travel on foot in the open through areas with lions, leopards, over a dozen species of deadly snakes, etc., they have to maintain high levels of awareness in order to survive. They find is amazing that their guests coming from westernized environments are so totally blind to what is happening around them.

This is because westernized people tend to have almost completely shut down the peripheral vision processing centers of their brains. Between TVs, computers, video games, and classrooms where they are punished for paying attention to anything but what is happening at the front of the room, children quickly learn to ignore this part of their visual field.

In fact, scientists have shown that if you watch a child’s brain with an fMRI while they are watching television you will find that the parts of their brains responsible for processing peripheral vision are almost completely shut down. By the time they are adults, this part of their visual system has mostly atrophied.

Another interesting point is that native peoples who are in their baseline state tend to stay in wide-angle vision and only pull into focused macular vision when a specific threat is detected that they need to focus on. This may explain why people in wide-angle vision find it easier to stay in an alpha brain state while those who live in macular vision find that this generally pulls them in the direction of being in beta brain state. When I have taught people to use wide-angle vision, breathe properly, and walk correctly (instead of in the heel-first method forced on us by modern footwear), they find that their ability to maintain a calm state and drop into meditation when needed is greatly enhanced.

-Ailin

comments.title: Hoodoo bath
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer, and thank you again for hosting this forum for questions. It's incredibly informative.

I have a few questions about the hoodoo bath:

1. When you say generally to redress without towelling off, do you mean soaking wet slide into our day clothes?
2. When you say it is distinct from our usual bathing habits, does that mean we should do it after any showering or other routine hygiene? As if we did it beforehand we could not redress without towelling.
3. Can it be done with a partner or must/should it be done singly? Can we share the water basin or no?
4. If a person does not do it daily, will they still derive benefit from it? Can it be done as a one-off or with a specific focus in mind?

Last but not least: what are the implications of someone not brought up in hoodoo culture practising this and other forms of hoodoo magic? I understand that culture and location are often highly important to the practice of various magical forms and I'm concerned that I would be trodding on or appropriating something that isn't culturally appropriate for me and I don't know how that relates to something folk as hoodoo.

I hope this is not too many questions all at once. Many thanks again,

- D

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo bath
replies.content:
1) Yes.

2) Yes.

3) Not at all -- you and a partner can do it together. This makes it easier to cleanse the energetically sensitive areas along the spine. You can use the same water.

4) It's usually done as a one-off, in case of specific need. You have to be in a lot of trouble before you need to do it daily.

5) Hoodoo isn't specific to any one American subculture. Its original roots are West African, but it had already absorbed influences from European and Native American cultural sources in colonial times, and it's done much more borrowing from every conceivable source since then. I didn't grow up with it; I learned it when I was in my forties, from a Jewish woman who learned it, beginning in her teen years, from (mostly) African-American teachers, but it's been practiced by people of every skin color in most parts of the US for a very long time.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo bath
replies.content: Thank you very much, I'm grateful for your response. I got the impression that it should be done daily from your response to "Questioning" in the April open post on your other blog, but re-reading it, it does seem like they were in a bit more trouble. Duly noted, and again, thank you for clarifying.
